column1: # Principle: Types are first-class. Type literals push as values; the
# test type bindings. None is the unit type, spelled `none`; its sole
# inhabitant IS the value `none`. The atom `null` is reserved for JSON
# This spec consolidates everything about types in aqleng:
#   1. Type-name words push type-literal values (Integer, String, …).
#      Type names are globally unique so the renderer prints just the
#   2. `none` parses to a None type literal. Rendering: "none".
#   3. `null` parses to an Atom("null") — the JSON-null value, NOT a
#   4. `type NAME body` installs a type binding (NAME starts [A-Z]).
#      Body can be a type literal, a record-shape map (`{x:Integer}`),
#   6. `typeof v` returns a Type literal — the type of v as a value.
#      For a concrete value, returns its exact VType (`typeof 5 →
#      Integer`). For a type literal, returns its metatype (`typeof
#      Integer → ScalarType`). For `none`, returns None itself.
#   7. `v is T` returns Boolean — does v satisfy type T?
#   8. Type literals are rejected at every concrete-payload sig slot
#      (anything other than TAny / metatype / matching TNone).
# Type literals are the language's value-level handle on its types —
# they're how you spell "the type Integer" or "the type List". Every
# reflective / generic operation (predicates, type equality, type
# unions, dispatch) builds on them. typeof + is together let user code
# branch on type at run time without resorting to ad-hoc string
# comparisons; the Type-typed return of typeof keeps the result
# composable (typeof's output IS a value usable by `is`).
# This is the aqleng (engine-kernel) subset. The richer type words
# (record, table, object, make, guard, inspect, base, convert, …) are
# layered on top in production aql; their semantics depend on parser /
# native-mod features that are intentionally out of aqleng scope.
#   addq     — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   subq     — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] - args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   concatq  — [String String] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (string concat)
#   firstq   — [List] forward-prec — first element or None for empty list
#   lengthq  — [List] forward-prec — list length as Integer
# A bare unquoted word that matches a type name is recognised by the
# engine (typeNames table in aqleng/go/engine.go) and resolves to a
# type-literal Value: VType = the named Type, Data = nil. The renderer
# prints just the leaf of the type path because type names are
# Leaf rendering — every type literal renders as its leaf.
# Family roots (Scalar / Node / Object) and the metatype root (Type)
# are themselves type-name words that resolve to type literals.
# Multiple type literals on the stack — each renders independently.
# Type literal next to a concrete value.
# def can bind a type literal as a simple value (the def-stack route).
# To get the Atom version of a type-name word, quote it.

column3: quote captures the Word as an Atom; type-name resolution doesn't fire

column1: # `none` is the single inhabitant of the None type. Its VType is
# TNone, Data == nil. Renders back as "none" via a dedicated branch in
# renderValue. `null`, by contrast, is reserved for JSON-null at the
# value level — the spec tokenizer parses it to Atom("null"), which

column3: bare none round-trips as the None type literal
None is its own "type" (no metatype lookup); typeof returns None

column1: # null is an atom, not a type literal.

column3: quoting an atom returns the atom unchanged

column1: # Dispatch — none at typed sigs fails (matcher rejects type literals);
# only Any-typed sigs accept it via the catch-all rule.

column3: addq wants [Number, Number]; None rejected at concrete slot

column1: # firstq on an empty list returns the None sentinel.

column3: none inside a list is a value
first element of a list whose head is none

column1: #   - none (the unique inhabitant of None) → None
#   - any other type literal (Data == nil) → its metatype
# typeof's result is a value that round-trips through `is`.
# Concrete scalars — typeof returns the EXACT type, not parts[1].
# Type literals — typeof reports the metatype.
# typeof's result IS a Type — round-trips:

column3: typeof none → None type literal; typeof None → metatype = Type (None has 1 part)

column1: # `type NAME body` validates and pushes (NAME, body) onto the type
# stack. NAME must start [A-Z]; body must satisfy IsTypeBody (a type
# literal, disjunct, implicit-map record shape, …). References to
# NAME resolve to the most-recent binding.

column3: Foo resolves to the Integer type literal

column1: # typeof resolves the Word to its bound type literal first.
type Foo Integer type Bar String Foo Bar
# An implicit-map literal `{name:Type ...}` is a structural record
# shape: a map whose keys carry expected types. type can install one
type R { x:Integer y:String } typeof R
column3: an implicit map is a Map; metatype is NodeType
the bound type literal renders as the implicit-map syntax

column1: type Point { x:Integer y:Integer } typeof Point
type Person { name:String age:Integer } typeof Person
# Nested-bracket record syntax — values can themselves be type literals.
type Nested { kind:String n:Number } typeof Nested
# NAME must start with a capital letter; otherwise type_error. Body

column3: digit first char also fails the capital-letter check
body Integer(42) is a concrete value, not a type body

column1: # def names start lowercase; type names start uppercase. The two

column3: x and X are different names; both succeed

column1: # `type` pushes; `untype` pops the most-recent. Shadowing reveals the
# previous binding when the top is popped.
type Foo Integer type Foo String Foo
column3: the String binding shadows the Integer one

column1: type Foo Integer type Foo String typeof Foo
column3: typeof on the shadow still reports ScalarType

column1: type Foo Integer type Foo String untype Foo Foo

column3: untype on the only binding empties the stack

column1: type Foo Integer untype Foo Foo
column3: Foo is no longer a registered type
no Foo on the type stack
lowercase name rejected before the lookup

column1: # `v is T` reads naturally: T is the upcoming forward word, v sits on
# Scalar concrete-value tests — true if v's VType matches T.

column3: Integer is a subtype of Number
Integer is NOT a subtype of Decimal

column1: # none is its own type — only matches None and Any.

column3: Integer's type path Scalar/Number/Integer matches Scalar/Number
Number itself is a Scalar subtype

column1: # When T is an implicit-map type literal, `is` checks each declared
# key is present in v with a matching field type.
type Point { x:Integer y:Integer } { x:1 y:2 } is Point
type Point { x:Integer y:Integer } { x:1 } is Point
type Point { x:Integer y:Integer } { x:1 y:"two" } is Point
type Point { x:Integer y:Integer } { x:1 y:2 z:3 } is Point
type Point { x:Integer y:Integer } [ 1 2 ] is Point
column3: v is a List, not a Map

column1: # 12. Dispatch — type literals are rejected at concrete-payload sigs
# Every concrete-payload sig slot rejects type literals (Data == nil).
# Type literals are still legitimately accepted at: TAny slots,
# metatype slots (Type/*), and the TNone slot for `none`.
# tagq has [Integer]→"specific" and [Any]→"any". Every type literal
# Arithmetic and string handlers reject type literals.

column3: core not has [TAny] fallback; CoerceBoolean(typelit) = false → true

column1: # List / Map type literals are rejected.
# Type literals at TAny continue to pass through.

column3: [TAny] sig; type literal accepted, handler returns metatype
